titleStmt.title: Letter from Neans bean í Rathgaille (Nancy O'Rahilly) to Mabel FitzGerald, circa 26 May 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Nancy O'Rahilly

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter is from Nancy O'Rahilly (1875-1961) to Mabel FitzGerald (née Washington McConnell) (1884-1958), wife of politician and intellectual Desmond FitzGerald (1888-1947). Nancy O'Rahilly writes says she hasn't been seeing people or answering letters because it upsets her and she is concerned for her unborn child and tries to reassure Mrs. FitzGerald saying that the prisoners (Desmond included) would be released at the end of the war. Mrs. O'Rahilly says that Desmond FitzGerald left his top coat at her house and she says that she has also heard about the bicycle being left in Milltown* and that she is having it collected. She refers to a note written by The O'Rahilly on Friday morning in which he praised Desmond highly. She is distraught but accepts her late husband's will to die for Ireland. Finally, she invites Mrs. FitzGerald to a Mass being offered for her late husband.Nancy O'Rahilly was a member of the Provisional Council of Cumann na mBan at the founding in 1914. She was the wife of Michael Joseph O'Rahilly, self-described as 'The' O'Rahilly (1875-1916) who was shot in Parnell/Moore Street on 28 April 1916 and died some time later. Nancy was pregnant at the time of her husband's death – the child was born three months later. Both Desmond FitzGerald and The O'Rahilly had attempted to stop the Easter Rising from taking place but joined in when they realised it was going ahead anyway.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Dear Mrs Fitzgerald I hope you will
forgive me for not seeing
you or answering your letters
but seeing people upsets
me so that I think for
the sake of the poor
little one coming I should
keep as quiet as possible.
Don't be worried about
Desmond's sentence.
Everyone is confident the
prisoners will be released
at the end of the war.
By the way he left his
top coat here, perhaps
you would like to send it
to him. I heard yesterday
that he had left the bicycle
in Milltown so I am sending
for it today. I had a little
note (written by Michael on
Friday morning) yesterday. One
of the girls brought it out.
He praised Desmond
greatly in it & said he
was invaluable to them.
I can't realizethe that he
will never come back it
seems too awful, but I
suppose he was quite
willing to give his life for
Ireland, I must try & keep
that before me. I am keeping wonderfully well
thank God. I suppose you
heard that Mrs. Burgess
had a little daughter.
Eileen was here the other
night & told Sarah. There is to be a Mass for
Michael in Donnybrook
on Tuesday the 30th at 10 a.m., perhaps
you would like to come.
I hope the children are better Is mise do cara Neans bean Ã­ Rathgaille
I had a letter from Miss
Allen, she was asking for
you all.
